questions.question: Which among the following is Volatile Memory in computer?
questions.explanation: Volatile memory is computer storage that only maintains its data while the device is powered. Most RAM (random access memory) used for primary storage in personal computers is volatile memory.

questions.question: 'Safari' is a type of
questions.explanation: Safari is a web browser developed by Apple based on the WebKit engine. First released in 2003 with Mac OS X Panther, a mobile version has been included in iOS devices since the introduction of the iPhone in 2007.

questions.question: An Integrated Circuit (IC) chip used in computers is made up of
questions.explanation: An integrated circuit (computer chip, or ic chip) is a piece of specially prepared silicon into which a very complex electronic circuit is etched using photographic techniques. Silicon chips can contain computer processors, memory and special devices. The chip is very fragile and so is normally surrounded by a tough plastic package, and electrical contact with the chip is provided through metal legs sticking out of the package.

questions.question: Which among the following is not an operating system of computers?
options: All the above are operating systems
questions.explanation: An operating system (OS) is system software that manages computer hardware and software resources and provides common services for computer programs. All computer programs, excluding firmware, require an operating system to function. Ex: Windows, Unix, Linux, Ubuntu, Mac OS X, etc.

questions.question: Which among the following is not an input device of computer?
questions.explanation: In computing, an input device is a (piece of computer hardware equipment) used to provide data and control signals to an information processing system such as a computer or information appliance. Examples of input devices include keyboards, mouse, scanners, digital cameras and joysticks. Plotter is an output device.

questions.question: What is the 'short cut' key combination for 'closing the currently active program in a computer'?
questions.explanation: Shortcut keys help provide an easier and usually quicker method of navigating and executing commands in computer software programs. 'Alt + F4' is the short cut key combination for closing the currently active program in a computer.

questions.question: Which of the following is a Low level computer language?
questions.explanation: Machine language is the language (in the form of 0's and 1's, called binary numbers) understood directly by the computer. It is machine dependent. It is difficult to learn and even more difficult to write programs. Low level languages: Machine language and Assembly language High level languages: COBOL, BASIC, FORTRAN, C, C++

questions.question: 'MICR' technology is commonly used by the banking industry to ease the processing and clearance of cheques and other documents. Expand MICR.
questions.explanation: Magnetic ink character recognition code (MICR Code) is a character-recognition technology used mainly by the banking industry to ease the processing and clearance of cheques and other documents. The MICR encoding, called the MICR line, is at the bottom of cheques and other vouchers and typically includes the document-type indicator, bank code, bank account number, cheque number, cheque amount, and a control indicator. The technology allows MICR readers to scan and read the information directly into a data-collection device. Unlike barcodes and similar technologies, MICR characters can be read easily by humans.

questions.question: Which of the following is an input device?
questions.explanation: Bar Code Reader is a device used for reading bar coded data (data in the form of light and dark lines). Bar coded data is generally used in labelling goods, numbering the books, etc. It may be a handheld scanner or may be embedded in a stationary scanner. Other three are output devices.

questions.question: Which among the following is not an application software?
questions.explanation: Application software is a set of programs, which are written to perform specific tasks, for example: An application package for managing library known as library information system is used to manage information of library such as: keeping book details, account holder details, book issue details, book return details etc. Linux is an operating system.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a database software?
questions.explanation: Database software: Database is a collection of related data. The purpose of this software is to organize and manage data. The advantage of this software is that you can change way data is stored and displayed. MS access, dBase, FoxPro, Paradox, and Oracle are some of the examples of database software. SmartSuite is an office suite from Lotus Software.

questions.question: Which computer language has been developed for solving mathematical and scientific problems?
questions.explanation: High level computer languages: • BASIC (Beginners All Purpose Symbolic Instruction Code): It is widely used, easy to learn general purpose language. Mainly used in microcomputers in earlier days. • COBOL (Common Business Oriented language): A standardized language used for commercial applications. • FORTRAN (Formula Translation): Developed for solving mathematical and scientific problems. One of the most popular languages among scientific community. • C: Structured Programming Language used for all purpose such as scientific application, commercial application, developing games etc. • C++: Popular object oriented programming language, used for general purpose.

questions.question: Arithmetic Logic Unit is a/an
questions.explanation: Functional Units of Computer: • Input Unit: This unit is used for entering data and programs into the computer system by the user for processing. • Storage Unit: The storage unit is used for storing data and instructions before and after processing. • Output Unit: The output unit is used for storing the result as output produced by the computer after processing.

questions.question: Centre for Excellence in Telecom Technology and Management (CETTM) located in
questions.explanation: Centre for Excellence in Telecom Technology and Management (CETTM) located in Hiranandani Gardens, Powai, Mumbai is the telecom training centre of MTNL, the incumbent operator of Government of India (GOI). It was founded in 2003 as one of the largest telecom training centres in India and amongst the biggest in Asia. The Centre provides extensive training to corporates and students besides MTNL's internal employees in telecom switching, transmission, wireless communication, telecom operations and management.

questions.question: An input device that converts letters, numbers and other characters into electrical signals readable by a processor is called
questions.explanation: In computing, a computer keyboard is a typewriter-style device which uses an arrangement of buttons or keys to act as a mechanical lever or electronic switch. Following the decline of punch cards and paper tape, interaction via teleprinter-style keyboards became the main input device for computers. A keyboard typically has characters engraved or printed on the keys (buttons) and each press of a key typically corresponds to a single written symbol. However, to produce some symbols requires pressing and holding several keys simultaneously or in sequence. While most keyboard keys produce letters, numbers or signs (characters), other keys or simultaneous key presses can produce actions or execute computer commands.

questions.question: The parity bit is added for
questions.explanation: A parity bit, or check bit, is a bit added to a string of binary code to ensure that the total number of 1-bits in the string is even or odd. Parity bits are used as the simplest form of error detecting code. There are two variants of parity bits: even parity bit and odd parity bit.

questions.question: What is the file extension for Microsoft PowerPoint?
questions.explanation: .pptx : PowerPoint Presentation - The default PowerPoint 2013, PowerPoint 2010, and Office PowerPoint 2007 XML-based file format.

questions.question: What are the two parts of an e-mail address?
questions.explanation: An email address identifies an email box to which email messages are delivered. A wide variety of formats were used in early email systems, but only a single format is used today, following the standards developed for Internet mail systems since the 1980s. An email address such as John.Smith@example.com is made up of a local-part(username), an @ symbol, then a case-insensitive domain. Although the standard specifies the local part to be case-sensitive, in practice the mail system at example.com may treat John.Smith as equivalent to JohnSmith or even as johnsmith, and mail systems often limit their users' choice of name to a subset of the technically valid characters. In some cases they also limit which addresses it is possible to send mail to.

questions.question: Technically what is chip?
questions.explanation: An integrated circuit or monolithic integrated circuit (also referred to as an IC, a chip, or a microchip) is a set of electronic circuits on one small flat piece (or "chip") of semiconductor material, normally silicon. The integration of large numbers of tiny transistors into a small chip results in circuits that are orders of magnitude smaller, cheaper, and faster than those constructed of discrete electronic components.

questions.question: What is the full form of FTP?
questions.explanation: The File Transfer Protocol (FTP) is a standard network protocol used for the transfer of computer files between a client and server on a computer network. FTP is built on a client-server model architecture and uses separate control and data connections between the client and the server. FTP users may authenticate themselves with a clear-text sign-in protocol, normally in the form of a username and password, but can connect anonymously if the server is configured to allow it. For secure transmission that protects the username and password, and encrypts the content, FTP is often secured with SSL/TLS (FTPS). SSH File Transfer Protocol (SFTP) is sometimes also used instead; it is technologically different.

questions.question: The Centre for Development of Telematics (C-DOT) is head-quartered in
questions.explanation: The Centre for Development of Telematics (C-DOT) is an Indian Government owned telecommunications technology development centre. It was established in 1984 with initial mandate of designing and developing digital exchanges. C-DOT has expanded to develop intelligent computer software applications. It is head-quartered in New Delhi, having offices in Bangalore and Kolkata. It is one of the 2 Indian Government organisations which have been appraised at Maturity Level 5 of CMMI-DEV v1.3, other being Bharat Electronics Limited (BEL) Software Technology Centre.

questions.question: The software that reads a program written in assembly language and translates it into an equivalent program in machine language is
questions.explanation: A computer will not understand any program written in a language, other than its machine language. The programs written in other languages must be translated into the machine language. Such translation is performed with the help of software. A program which translates an assembly language program into a machine language program is called an assembler. If an assembler which runs on a computer and produces the machine codes for the same computer then it is called self-assembler or resident assembler. If an assembler that runs on a computer and produces the machine codes for other computer then it is called Cross Assembler.

questions.question: The sharing of a medium and its path by two or more devices is called
questions.explanation: In telecommunications and computer networks, multiplexing (sometimes contracted to muxing) is a method by which multiple analog or digital signals are combined into one signal over a shared medium. The aim is to share a scarce resource. For example, in telecommunications, several telephone calls may be carried using one wire. Multiplexing originated in telegraphy in the 1870s, and is now widely applied in communications. In telephony, George Owen Squier is credited with the development of telephone carrier multiplexing in 1910.

questions.question: In MS PowerPoint, which of the following keys can be used to view the slide show?
questions.explanation: • F1: Display Help or the Office Assistant • F2: Select the text box (with text or an object selected inside the text box) or select the text within a text box (with the text box selected) • F4: Repeat the last action • F5: Carry out Slide Show command • F6: Move to the next pane

questions.question: In MS Excel, Freeze Pane option can be seen in
questions.explanation: To keep an area of a worksheet visible while you scroll to another area of the worksheet, go to the View tab, where you can Freeze Panes to lock specific rows and columns in place, or you can Split panes to create separate windows of the same worksheet.

questions.question: The name assigned by user to a document is called as
questions.explanation: A filename (also written as two words, file name) is a name used to uniquely identify a computer file stored in a file system. Different file systems impose different restrictions on filename lengths and the allowed characters within filenames.

questions.question: What 'I' stands in EDI?
questions.explanation: Electronic Data Interchange (EDI) is the electronic interchange of business information using a standardized format; a process which allows one company to send information to another company electronically rather than with paper. Business entities conducting business electronically are called trading partners.

questions.question: In 102, which effect has been applied to raise the letter '2'?
questions.explanation: Abbreviated as sup, a superscript is a character(s) that is half the height of a standard character, and printed higher than the rest of the text. An example of superscript is shown below. Normal TextSuperscript Text

questions.question: Zipping of file is a technique of
questions.explanation: ZIP is an archive file format that supports lossless data compression. A.ZIP file may contain one or more files or directories that may have been compressed. The.ZIP file format permits a number of compression algorithms, though DEFLATE is the most common. This format was originally created in 1989 by Phil Katz, and was first implemented in PKWARE, Inc.'s PKZIP utility, as a replacement for the previous ARC compression format by Thom Henderson.

questions.question: 1 gigabyte =
questions.explanation: The gigabyte is a multiple of the unit byte for digital information. The unit symbol for the gigabyte is GB. 1GB is equivalent to 2014 Megabytes.
